text: Maya has a chocolate bar broken into 4 equal pieces. She eats 1 piece. What fraction of the chocolate bar did she eat?
explanation: 🍫 The bar is broken into 4 equal pieces.🍽️ Maya ate 1 piece.📐 A fraction means: pieces eaten ÷ total pieces = 1 ÷ 4 = 1/4.💡 Think of it as: "1 out of 4 pieces." That's one-quarter!

text: A pizza is cut into 8 equal slices. Leo eats 2 slices. What fraction of the pizza did Leo eat?
explanation: 🍕 The pizza has 8 slices in total.😋 Leo ate 2 slices.📐 Fraction = slices eaten ÷ total slices = 2 ÷ 8 = 2/8.💡 Bonus: 2/8 is the same as 1/4 because 2 goes into 8 four times. Cool, right?

text: A ribbon is divided into 3 equal parts. Sam colors 1 part red. What fraction of the ribbon is red?
explanation: 🎀 The ribbon is cut into 3 equal parts.🖍️ Sam colored 1 part red.📐 Fraction = colored parts ÷ total parts = 1 ÷ 3 = 1/3.💡 Imagine cutting a sandwich into 3 equal pieces and eating one — you ate 1/3!

text: Zara has 6 grapes. She gives 3 grapes to her friend. What fraction of her grapes did she give away?
explanation: 🍇 Zara started with 6 grapes.🤝 She gave away 3 grapes.📐 Fraction = grapes given ÷ total = 3 ÷ 6 = 3/6.💡 3 is exactly half of 6, so 3/6 = 1/2. She gave away half her grapes!

text: A garden is split into 5 equal rows. Flowers grow in 2 rows. What fraction of the garden has flowers?
explanation: 🌷 The garden has 5 rows in total.🌸 Flowers grow in 2 of those rows.📐 Fraction = flower rows ÷ total rows = 2 ÷ 5 = 2/5.💡 Think of 5 lines on your notebook — color 2 of them. That colored part is 2/5!

text: Emma reads 1/3 of her book on Monday and 1/3 on Tuesday. What fraction of the book has she read in total?
explanation: 📖 Monday: Emma read 1/3 of the book.📖 Tuesday: Emma read another 1/3.➕ When the bottom numbers (denominators) are the same, just add the top numbers: 1 + 1 = 2. Keep the 3 on the bottom.✅ Total read = 2/3 of the book!

text: A water tank is 3/4 full. After some water is used, it is only 1/4 full. What fraction of the water was used?
explanation: 💧 Tank starts: 3/4 full.💧 Tank after using water: 1/4 full.➖ To find how much was used, subtract: 3/4 − 1/4. Bottom numbers are the same, so subtract the tops: 3 − 1 = 2. Keep the 4.✅ Water used = 2/4 (same as 1/2 — half the tank was used!).

text: Ben jogs every 2 days. Lily jogs every 3 days. If they both jogged today, after how many days will they jog together again?
explanation: 🏃 Ben jogs every 2 days: Day 2, 4, 6, 8, 10...🏃 Lily jogs every 3 days: Day 3, 6, 9, 12...🔍 Look for the first day that appears in BOTH lists: that's Day 6!💡 This is called the LCM (Least Common Multiple). LCM(2, 3) = 6.

text: Two buses leave the same stop. Bus A comes every 4 minutes, Bus B every 6 minutes. If both left together at 8:00 AM, when will they next leave together?
explanation: 🚌 Bus A leaves every 4 min: minute 4, 8, 12, 16...🚌 Bus B leaves every 6 min: minute 6, 12, 18...🔍 First minute in both lists = 12 minutes. That's LCM(4, 6) = 12.🕗 8:00 AM + 12 minutes = 8:12 AM. See you then!

text: A recipe needs 1/2 cup of sugar. Priya wants to make 3 batches. How much sugar does she need in total?
explanation: 🍪 1 batch needs 1/2 cup of sugar.✖️ 3 batches need: 1/2 × 3 = 3/2 cups.💡 3/2 means "3 halves." 2 halves = 1 whole cup, plus 1 more half = 1 and 1/2 cups.🥄 Imagine 3 half-cup scoops poured in — that's one and a half cups!

text: A class has 20 students. 1/4 of them brought their lunch from home. How many students brought lunch from home?
explanation: 🎒 There are 20 students in the class.📐 "1/4 of 20" means: split 20 into 4 equal groups.➗ 20 ÷ 4 = 5.🥪 So 5 students brought lunch from home. (Imagine 4 groups of 5 kids — one group has their lunch box!)

text: Jake scores 3/5 on his first quiz and 4/5 on his second quiz. Which quiz did he do better on, and by how much?
explanation: 📝 Quiz 1: Jake scored 3/5.📝 Quiz 2: Jake scored 4/5.🔍 Same bottom number (5), so compare the tops: 4 > 3, so Quiz 2 is better.➖ How much better? 4/5 − 3/5 = 1/5. Jake improved by one-fifth!

text: A lamp flashes every 3 seconds. A bell rings every 5 seconds. They go off together at the start. After how many seconds will they go off together again?
explanation: 💡 Lamp flashes at: 3, 6, 9, 12, 15 seconds...🔔 Bell rings at: 5, 10, 15 seconds...🔍 First second in both lists = 15 seconds! That's LCM(3, 5) = 15.💡 Tip: LCM means the smallest number that both numbers divide into evenly.

text: Nora ate 1/4 of a pie. Her brother ate 2/4 of the same pie. What fraction of the pie is left?
explanation: 🥧 Nora ate 1/4 of the pie. Brother ate 2/4.➕ Total eaten: 1/4 + 2/4 = 3/4 (same denominators, add the tops: 1+2=3).➖ Whole pie = 4/4. Left over = 4/4 − 3/4 = 1/4.💡 Picture a pie cut into 4 slices: Nora takes 1, brother takes 2. Only 1 slice left!

text: Three friends share a bag of 12 cookies equally. Then one friend shares her portion equally with her little sister. What fraction of the total cookies does the little sister get?
explanation: 🍪 12 cookies shared equally among 3 friends: 12 ÷ 3 = 4 cookies each (that's 1/3 of the bag).👧 One friend shares her 4 cookies equally with her little sister: 4 ÷ 2 = 2 cookies for the sister.📐 Sister's fraction = 2 out of 12 total = 2/12 = 1/6.💡 Splitting a third in half always gives you a sixth: 1/3 ÷ 2 = 1/6!

text: A train stops at Oak Station every 6 minutes and at Elm Station every 9 minutes. Both trains left at noon. At what time will they next leave their stations at the same moment?
explanation: 🚆 Oak Station train leaves every 6 min: minute 6, 12, 18, 24...🚆 Elm Station train leaves every 9 min: minute 9, 18, 27...🔍 First minute in both lists = 18 minutes. That's LCM(6, 9) = 18.🕛 12:00 noon + 18 minutes = 12:18 PM. Both trains leave at the same time!

text: Mia ran 3/8 of a race before lunch and 2/8 of the race after lunch. Tom ran 5/8 of the same race. Who ran more, and by what fraction?
choices: Tom by 0 — they ran the same
answer: Tom by 0 — they ran the same
explanation: 🏃 Mia ran 3/8 before lunch and 2/8 after lunch.➕ Add them: 3/8 + 2/8 = 5/8 (same denominators — just add the tops: 3+2=5).🏃 Tom ran 5/8.⚖️ Mia's total = 5/8. Tom's total = 5/8. They are exactly equal — it's a tie! 🤝

text: A farmer plants sunflowers in 2/6 of his field and roses in 3/6 of his field. The rest is grass. What fraction of the field is grass?
explanation: 🌻 Sunflowers: 2/6 of the field. 🌹 Roses: 3/6 of the field.➕ Total plants: 2/6 + 3/6 = 5/6 (same denominator — add the tops: 2+3=5).🌿 The whole field = 6/6. Grass = 6/6 − 5/6 = 1/6.💡 Imagine 6 equal strips of field — 2 have sunflowers, 3 have roses, and only 1 has grass!

text: Three school bells ring at intervals of 4, 6, and 8 minutes. All three rang together at 9:00 AM. What is the next time all three bells will ring together?
explanation: 🔔 Bell 1 rings every 4 min: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24...🔔 Bell 2 rings every 6 min: 6, 12, 18, 24...🔔 Bell 3 rings every 8 min: 8, 16, 24...🔍 First number in all three lists = 24. LCM(4, 6, 8) = 24.🕘 9:00 AM + 24 minutes = 9:24 AM. All three bells ring together again!

text: A bookshelf has 30 books. 1/3 are science books, 2/5 are story books, and the rest are art books. How many art books are there?
explanation: 📚 Science books: 1/3 of 30 = 30 ÷ 3 = 10 books.📖 Story books: 2/5 of 30 = 30 ÷ 5 × 2 = 12 books.➕ Science + Stories = 10 + 12 = 22 books.➖ Art books = 30 − 22 = 8 books.💡 No need for tricky fractions here — just find each group's count and subtract!

text: A jar is 3/4 full of juice. You pour out 1/3 of the juice in the jar. What fraction of a full jar is left?
explanation: 🫙 The jar has 3/4 of juice inside it.💧 We pour out 1/3 of the 3/4 that's there — multiply: 1/3 × 3/4 = 3/12 = 1/4 poured out.➖ Juice left = 3/4 − 1/4 = 2/4 = 1/2.💡 When you take a fraction OF a fraction, you multiply them. "Of" means multiply!

text: In a class, 1/2 of the kids like soccer, 1/3 like basketball, and the rest like tennis. If there are 30 students, how many like tennis?
explanation: ⚽ Soccer: 1/2 of class. 🏀 Basketball: 1/3 of class.➕ To add 1/2 + 1/3, we need the same denominator. LCM(2,3) = 6. So: 1/2 = 3/6 and 1/3 = 2/6.➕ 3/6 + 2/6 = 5/6 like soccer or basketball.🎾 Tennis fans = 1 − 5/6 = 1/6 of the class. 1/6 of 30 = 30 ÷ 6 = 5 students!

text: Three friends share a pizza. Ana eats 1/3, Bob eats 1/4, and Carol eats 1/6. What fraction of the pizza is left?
explanation: 🍕 We need to add 1/3 + 1/4 + 1/6. Different bottoms — we need a common denominator.🔢 LCM(3, 4, 6) = 12. Convert: 1/3 = 4/12, 1/4 = 3/12, 1/6 = 2/12.➕ Total eaten: 4/12 + 3/12 + 2/12 = 9/12.➖ Left = 12/12 − 9/12 = 3/12 = 1/4. One quarter of the pizza remains!

text: A frog jumps every 5 seconds. A cricket chirps every 8 seconds. A firefly blinks every 10 seconds. All happen at the same time at t=0. After how many seconds do all three happen at the same time again?
explanation: 🐸 Frog jumps at: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40...🦗 Cricket chirps at: 8, 16, 24, 32, 40...✨ Firefly blinks at: 10, 20, 30, 40...🔍 First second in all three lists = 40 seconds. LCM(5, 8, 10) = 40!

text: A school garden has 4 sections. Section A takes up 2/8, section B takes 3/8, section C takes 2/8. What fraction does section D take up?
explanation: 🌱 Sections A, B, C: 2/8 + 3/8 + 2/8. Same denominator — add the tops: 2+3+2 = 7.➕ Sections A+B+C = 7/8 of the garden.➖ The whole garden = 8/8. Section D = 8/8 − 7/8 = 1/8.💡 Think of 8 equal garden squares: A gets 2, B gets 3, C gets 2 — only 1 square is left for D!

text: Two cyclists start at the same point. Cyclist A completes a lap every 8 minutes, Cyclist B every 12 minutes. How many laps will each have completed the first time they meet at the start?
explanation: 🚴 Cyclist A finishes every 8 min: minute 8, 16, 24...🚴 Cyclist B finishes every 12 min: minute 12, 24...🔍 First minute in both = 24 minutes. LCM(8, 12) = 24.🏁 At 24 minutes: A did 24÷8 = 3 laps. B did 24÷12 = 2 laps. They meet at the start!

text: Lily has 24 stickers. She gives 1/3 to her sister and 1/4 to her cousin. How many stickers does Lily have left?
explanation: 🌟 Lily has 24 stickers.👧 Gives 1/3 to sister: 24 ÷ 3 = 8 stickers.👦 Gives 1/4 to cousin: 24 ÷ 4 = 6 stickers.➖ Total given = 8 + 6 = 14. Lily keeps: 24 − 14 = 10 stickers!

text: A baker makes cookies in batches of 8, muffins in batches of 6, and brownies in batches of 4. He wants to bake the same number of each treat. What is the least number of each treat he should bake?
explanation: 🍪 Cookies come in batches of 8: 8, 16, 24...🧁 Muffins come in batches of 6: 6, 12, 18, 24...🍫 Brownies come in batches of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24...🔍 First number in all three lists = 24. LCM(8, 6, 4) = 24. The baker needs 24 of each!

text: A water fountain fills 2/5 of a tank in the morning and 1/4 of the tank in the afternoon. A small crack leaks 1/10 of the tank per day. Will the tank be more than half full by end of day? By how much?
choices: No, it is 1/20 short of half
explanation: 🔢 Use 20 as the common denominator (LCM of 5, 4, 10 = 20).🌅 Morning fill: 2/5 = 8/20. 🌇 Afternoon fill: 1/4 = 5/20.💧 Leak: 1/10 = 2/20.🧮 Total = 8/20 + 5/20 − 2/20 = 11/20. Half the tank = 10/20.✅ Since 11 > 10, the tank is more than half full — by 1/20!

text: Four friends run laps. Amir finishes a lap every 6 min, Beni every 9 min, Cora every 12 min, and Dana every 18 min. They all start together. What is the shortest time until all four are at the start together, and how many laps has each completed?
choices: After 36 min — Amir:6, Beni:4, Cora:3, Dana:2
After 36 min — Amir:4, Beni:3, Cora:2, Dana:1
After 18 min — Amir:3, Beni:2, Cora:1, Dana:1
After 36 min — Amir:6, Beni:3, Cora:4, Dana:2
answer: After 36 min — Amir:6, Beni:4, Cora:3, Dana:2
explanation: 🔢 Find LCM(6, 9, 12, 18): multiples of 18 = 18, 36. Does 36 work for all? 36÷6=6 ✅ 36÷9=4 ✅ 36÷12=3 ✅ 36÷18=2 ✅⏱️ They all meet again after 36 minutes.🏃 Amir: 36÷6 = 6 laps. Beni: 36÷9 = 4 laps.🏃 Cora: 36÷12 = 3 laps. Dana: 36÷18 = 2 laps. Everyone arrives together!
